review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for two architecture-focused questions on LLaVA and Flamingo.

question_bank.question: What is the purpose of the trainable connector in a LLaVA-style model?
choices: To map CLIP visual features into a representation space that the language model can consume
To decode the final text output back into image patches
To replace the need for a text tokenizer inside the language model
To supervise the CLIP encoder with a segmentation loss
question_bank.explanation: CLIP features are useful visual representations, but they are not automatically in the right format for an LLM. The connector learns how to translate them into the token space the language model can use.

question_bank.question: How does Flamingo differ from LLaVA in how it uses vision features?
choices: Flamingo injects vision information into LLM layers through perceiver sampling and gated cross-attention
Flamingo removes the language model and predicts only image embeddings
Flamingo uses only a CLS token and discards all patch features
Flamingo trains a new CLIP model from scratch for each downstream task
question_bank.explanation: Instead of only passing visual tokens at the input, Flamingo lets the language model repeatedly access vision features throughout its layers, which gives more flexible cross-modal interaction.
